introHtml: Node.js interviews test your understanding of single-threaded non-blocking I/O. The event loop phases, stream backpressure, cluster vs worker threads, and production patterns like graceful shutdown are common targets. Knowing libuv internals at a conceptual level is a strong differentiator.

topics.bodyHtml:
Phases of the Node.js Event Loop

// 1. timers:          setTimeout, setInterval callbacks
// 2. pending:         I/O callbacks from previous iteration
// 3. idle/prepare:    internal use
// 4. poll:            retrieve new I/O events (blocking if queue empty)
// 5. check:           setImmediate callbacks
// 6. close callbacks: socket.on("close"), etc.

// Between each phase: process.nextTick + Promise microtasks drain

setImmediate vs setTimeout(fn, 0)

// In the main module: order is non-deterministic (system/timing dependent)
setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout"), 0);
setImmediate(() => console.log("immediate"));
// May output either order

// Inside I/O callback: setImmediate ALWAYS runs first
const fs = require("fs");
fs.readFile("file.txt", () => {
  setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout"), 0);
  setImmediate(() => console.log("immediate")); // always first
});

// process.nextTick: runs BEFORE next event loop phase (before I/O)
process.nextTick(() => console.log("nextTick"));
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("promise"));
setImmediate(() => console.log("setImmediate"));
setTimeout(() => console.log("setTimeout"), 0);

// Output: nextTick, promise, setTimeout/setImmediate (order varies), setImmediate

// libuv thread pool (default 4 threads, UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE)
// Used by: fs (most ops), crypto (pbkdf2, randomBytes), dns.lookup, zlib

fs

const fs = require("fs");
const fsp = require("fs/promises");

// Sync (blocks event loop — avoid in production servers)
const data = fs.readFileSync("file.txt", "utf8");

// Callback-based
fs.readFile("file.txt", "utf8", (err, data) => {
  if (err) throw err;
  console.log(data);
});

// Promise-based (preferred)
const data = await fsp.readFile("file.txt", "utf8");
await fsp.writeFile("out.txt", content, "utf8");
await fsp.appendFile("log.txt", line + "\n");
const files = await fsp.readdir("./src");
const stat = await fsp.stat("file.txt");
await fsp.mkdir("./dist", { recursive: true });

// Streaming (for large files)
const readable = fs.createReadStream("large.csv");
const writable = fs.createWriteStream("out.csv");
readable.pipe(writable);

path

const path = require("path");
path.join("src", "utils", "index.js");  // "src/utils/index.js" (OS separator)
path.resolve("./src", "utils");         // absolute path
path.dirname("/foo/bar/file.js");       // "/foo/bar"
path.basename("/foo/bar/file.js");      // "file.js"
path.basename("/foo/bar/file.js", ".js"); // "file"
path.extname("file.js");               // ".js"
path.parse("/foo/bar/file.js");
// { root: "/", dir: "/foo/bar", base: "file.js", ext: ".js", name: "file" }
__dirname; // directory of current file (CJS only)
__filename; // current file path (CJS only)

crypto

const crypto = require("crypto");

// Hashing
crypto.createHash("sha256").update("data").digest("hex");

// HMAC
crypto.createHmac("sha256", secret).update("data").digest("hex");

// Random bytes
const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");

// Password hashing (use bcrypt or argon2, not crypto directly)
// bcrypt: adaptive, slow by design
import bcrypt from "bcrypt";
const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, 12); // saltRounds=12
const match = await bcrypt.compare(password, hash);

child_process

MethodUse caseBuffers outputStreams
execShell commands, small outputyesno
spawnLong-running processes, large outputnoyes
execFileExecute binary (no shell)yesno
forkNode.js child process with IPCnoIPC channel
const { exec, spawn } = require("child_process");

exec("ls -la", (err, stdout, stderr) => console.log(stdout));

const ls = spawn("ls", ["-la"]);
ls.stdout.on("data", data => console.log(data.toString()));
ls.on("close", code => console.log(`exited: ${code}`));

EventEmitter

const { EventEmitter } = require("events");

class MyEmitter extends EventEmitter {}
const emitter = new MyEmitter();

emitter.on("data", (chunk) => process(chunk));
emitter.once("connect", () => console.log("connected once"));
emitter.emit("data", Buffer.from("hello"));
emitter.removeListener("data", handler);
emitter.removeAllListeners("data");

// Memory leak warning: default maxListeners = 10
emitter.setMaxListeners(20);

const { Readable, Writable, Transform, pipeline } = require("stream");

// Why streams: process data in chunks instead of loading entirely into memory
// 1GB file: buffering needs 1GB RAM; streaming needs ~64KB

// Readable stream
const { createReadStream } = require("fs");
const readable = createReadStream("big.file");
readable.on("data", chunk => process(chunk));
readable.on("end", () => console.log("done"));
readable.on("error", err => console.error(err));

// Backpressure: writable signals it's full → readable pauses
// pipe handles backpressure automatically
readable.pipe(writable);

// Transform stream
const { createGzip } = require("zlib");
const gzip = createGzip();
readable.pipe(gzip).pipe(writable); // compress on the fly

// pipeline: better than pipe (handles errors + cleanup)
const { promisify } = require("util");
const pipelineAsync = promisify(pipeline);
await pipelineAsync(createReadStream("input"), createGzip(), createWriteStream("output.gz"));

// Custom transform
const upperCase = new Transform({
  transform(chunk, encoding, callback) {
    callback(null, chunk.toString().toUpperCase());
  }
});

const express = require("express");
const app = express();

// Middleware: (req, res, next) => void
// Runs in order of registration
app.use(express.json());          // parse JSON body
app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // parse form data

// Custom middleware
const logger = (req, res, next) => {
  console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url}`);
  next(); // MUST call next() or send response
};
app.use(logger);

// Router organization
const userRouter = express.Router();
userRouter.get("/", listUsers);
userRouter.get("/:id", getUser);
userRouter.post("/", createUser);
userRouter.put("/:id", updateUser);
userRouter.delete("/:id", deleteUser);
app.use("/api/users", userRouter);

// Error-handling middleware: MUST have 4 args
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  console.error(err.stack);
  res.status(err.statusCode || 500).json({
    error: err.message || "Internal Server Error"
  });
});

// Async wrapper to catch errors
const asyncHandler = fn => (req, res, next) => Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next);
app.get("/users/:id", asyncHandler(async (req, res) => {
  const user = await User.findById(req.params.id);
  if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ error: "Not found" });
  res.json(user);
}));

// Security middleware
import helmet from "helmet";
import cors from "cors";
import rateLimit from "express-rate-limit";
app.use(helmet());
app.use(cors({ origin: ["https://myapp.com"], credentials: true }));
app.use(rateLimit({ windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, max: 100 }));

Sessions vs JWT

FeatureSessionsJWT
StorageServer (DB/Redis)Client (cookie/localStorage)
RevocationEasy (delete session)Hard (need blacklist or short TTL)
ScalabilityNeeds shared session storeStateless — easy to scale
SizeSmall cookieLarger token
Best forWeb apps, need instant revokeAPIs, microservices, mobile

JWT Implementation

import jwt from "jsonwebtoken";

// Sign (create)
const token = jwt.sign(
  { userId: user.id, role: user.role },  // payload
  process.env.JWT_SECRET,                 // secret
  { expiresIn: "15m", algorithm: "HS256" }
);

// Verify
try {
  const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
  req.user = decoded;
  next();
} catch (err) {
  if (err.name === "TokenExpiredError") return res.status(401).json({ error: "Token expired" });
  return res.status(401).json({ error: "Invalid token" });
}

// Refresh token pattern
// Access token: short-lived (15min), stored in memory
// Refresh token: long-lived (7d), stored in HttpOnly cookie
// Rotation: each refresh issues new refresh token + invalidates old

// JWT vulnerabilities:
// 1. alg: "none" attack → always verify algorithm explicitly
// 2. Weak secrets → use 256-bit random secret
// 3. Sensitive data in payload → payload is base64, NOT encrypted
// 4. Long expiry → use short access tokens + refresh tokens

// Operational errors: expected (invalid input, DB down) — handle gracefully
// Programmer errors: bugs (TypeError, undefined.property) — let it crash

// Custom error class
class AppError extends Error {
  constructor(message, statusCode = 500, code = "INTERNAL_ERROR") {
    super(message);
    this.statusCode = statusCode;
    this.code = code;
    this.isOperational = true;
  }
}

// Async wrapper
const asyncHandler = fn => (req, res, next) =>
  Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next);

// Unhandled rejections (Node.js)
process.on("unhandledRejection", (reason, promise) => {
  console.error("Unhandled Rejection at:", promise, "reason:", reason);
  // Graceful shutdown
  server.close(() => process.exit(1));
});

process.on("uncaughtException", (err) => {
  console.error("Uncaught Exception:", err);
  process.exit(1); // Required — state is undefined
});

// PM2: process manager
// pm2 start app.js -i max    -- cluster mode (one process per CPU)
// pm2 reload app             -- zero-downtime reload
// pm2 monit                  -- monitoring dashboard

// Cluster module
const cluster = require("cluster");
const os = require("os");

if (cluster.isPrimary) {
  os.cpus().forEach(() => cluster.fork());
  cluster.on("exit", (worker) => {
    console.log(`Worker ${worker.id} died, forking new`);
    cluster.fork();
  });
} else {
  require("./app"); // each worker runs app
}

// Connection pooling with pg
import { Pool } from "pg";
const pool = new Pool({ max: 20, idleTimeoutMillis: 30000 });
const client = await pool.connect();
try {
  const result = await client.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", [id]);
} finally {
  client.release(); // Always release!
}

// NODE_ENV production effects:
// - Express: disables dev error messages
// - React: no dev warnings, smaller bundle
// - Many libraries optimize behavior

items.question: If Node is single-threaded, what work is actually happening off the main thread?
items.answerHtml: JavaScript execution is single-threaded in one event loop, but libuv uses a worker pool for many filesystem, crypto, DNS, and compression tasks. The OS also handles network I/O asynchronously. So Node is single-threaded for JS, not for all work in the process.
items.flashcardBackHtml: JavaScript execution is single-threaded, but libuv and the OS handle many operations concurrently.

items.question: What is backpressure in streams, and why is it important in production systems?
items.answerHtml: Backpressure is the mechanism that slows a producer when the consumer cannot keep up. Without it, memory usage grows because data buffers faster than it can be processed. Streams and pipe help coordinate flow so large files and responses do not overwhelm RAM.
items.flashcardBackHtml: It is the flow-control signal that prevents producers from overwhelming slower consumers.

items.question: How do `process.nextTick`, promise microtasks, `setImmediate`, and `setTimeout` differ in Node?
items.answerHtml: process.nextTick runs before the event loop continues, even before other microtasks in many practical explanations of Node behavior. Promise callbacks are microtasks. setImmediate runs in the check phase, while setTimeout runs in the timers phase. Inside I/O callbacks, setImmediate often wins predictably.

items.question: When would you choose worker threads, cluster, or child processes?
items.answerHtml: Use worker threads for CPU-bound JavaScript work that should share the process more cheaply than full OS processes. Use cluster to scale HTTP handling across CPU cores with multiple Node processes. Use child processes when you need isolation, another runtime, or shell/binary execution.

items.question: Why is synchronous filesystem or crypto work dangerous in a request handler?
items.answerHtml: It blocks the event loop, which means no other request callbacks can run during that time. Even small synchronous operations can create latency spikes under concurrency because one blocked loop stalls all in-flight requests handled by that process.

items.question: How would you explain graceful shutdown in a Node service?
items.answerHtml: Stop accepting new traffic, let in-flight requests finish within a timeout, close database or queue connections, flush logs if needed, and then exit. In Kubernetes or process managers this usually starts with handling SIGTERM properly instead of killing the process abruptly.

items.question: Your Node API shows high CPU even though the database is healthy. What would you investigate first?
items.answerHtml: Look for CPU-heavy JSON parsing, large regex work, hot loops, synchronous crypto/compression, huge in-memory transforms, or excessive logging. I would also capture a CPU profile to confirm whether the bottleneck is userland code, garbage collection, or native bindings.

items.question: Uploads sometimes fail under load and memory spikes sharply. How would streams change the design?
items.answerHtml: Move from buffering whole payloads in memory to streaming from request to storage or a transform pipeline. That keeps memory bounded per connection and lets backpressure slow the sender naturally instead of crashing the process with unbounded buffers.

items.question: An Express app occasionally returns duplicate error responses or hangs forever. What middleware mistakes would you check?
items.answerHtml: I would check for missing return statements after sending responses, forgetting to call next(), calling next() after a response was already sent, and async handlers that throw without being wrapped. Middleware order and the four-argument signature for error handlers are common failure points.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: Node's event loop has 6 phases; setImmediate fires in the check phase, process.nextTick fires between any two phases
Streams solve the memory problem of loading entire files/responses into RAM — always prefer streams for large data
Never block the event loop with synchronous CPU work; offload to worker threads or child processes
cluster spawns multiple processes sharing the same port; each has its own V8 instance and memory space
Unhandled promise rejections must be caught globally with process.on('unhandledRejection')
Express middleware order matters; error-handling middleware must have exactly 4 parameters: (err, req, res, next)
Always use connection pooling for database clients — never create a new connection per request
